macro_segments.title: The Status of Night as a Time for Wearing Tefillin: Between Halacha and Rabbinic Concern

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Core Question of Whether Night is a Designated Time for Tefillin
Distinguishing Between Halachic Categories: When Night Definitively Begins Versus Rabbinic Concerns About Sleep
The Conceptual Question: Is the Prohibition Rooted in Torah or Rabbinic Concern About Bodily Functions
Comparative Analysis: Why Tefillin Differs from Lulav and Tzitzit in Terms of Wearing at Non-Designated Times
The Action-Versus-State Distinction: How the Verbs Ukshartam and Vehayu Frame the Obligation as Binding Versus Wearing
Kedusha and Boundaries: Why Unlimited Time for a Mitzvah Undermines Its Sacred Status

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Contradiction: How Rabba bar Rav Huna's Position on Night Tefillin Reconciles with His Stringency on Shabbat

micro_segments.title: The Apparent Contradiction: If Night is a Permissible Time, Why Must Tefillin Be Removed on Friday Night
Resolution Through Context: Erev Shabbat as the Critical Juncture Where Shabbat Itself (Not Mere Night) Requires Removal
Examining Rabba bar Rav Huna's Actual Position: Proof That He Held Night Is a Permitted Time from His Lenient Ruling on Safek Chasheicha

macro_segments.title: The Scriptural Derivation: How Parashat Kadesh Excludes Both Night and Shabbat from the Time for Tefillin

micro_segments.title: The Source Text: Veshamarta et Hachukim Hazot L'Moada Mimim Yamima and Its Use to Exclude Non-Daytime Periods
The Dispute Between Rabbi Yosi HaGlili and Rabbi Akiva: Is the Pasuk Teaching About Tefillin or Exclusively About Korban Pesach
Reconciling the Sources: How Both Views on the Pasuk's Scope Lead to Excluding Shabbat and Yom Tov from Tefillin Obligation

macro_segments.title: The Ot (Sign) Framework: Why Shabbat and Yom Tov Themselves Serve as Signs, Making Separate Tefillin Obsolete

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's Derivation: When a Sign is Needed as a Sign, Days of Rest That Are Inherently Significant Do Not Require Additional Marking
The Nature of Ot: How Constant Reminders Undermine the Function of a Sign; Comparison to the Tzitz of the Kohen Gadol
Tosafot's Analysis: The Ot as Mitzva-Specific Reminders; How Chol HaMoed Complicates the Category of Days Requiring or Not Requiring Tefillin
Expansion to Yom Tov: Using the Logic of the Ot to Argue Whether Chol HaMoed Also Excludes Tefillin Based on Its Own Commemorative Character

macro_segments.title: Practical Rulings and Historical Practices: Halacha vs. Minhag on When to Wear or Refrain from Tefillin on Festival Days

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Position: Tefillin Is Not Worn on Shabbat and Yom Tov; Chol HaMoed Remains Unresolved in the Gemara Itself
Ashkenazi Minhag: Refraining from Tefillin on Chol HaMoed; Sephardi Variations and the Question of Bracha
Personal Practice Strategy: Wearing Tefillin Without Bracha on Chol HaMoed as a Middle Path to Fulfill the Mitzva Without Desecrating the Day

macro_segments.title: The Obligation to Maintain Awareness: Rabbi Elazar and the Requirement of Constant Consciousness While Wearing Tefillin

micro_segments.title: The Prohibition: Taking Off Tefillin After Sunset Is a Transgression of Veshamarta; Rabbi Elazar's Ruling
The Exception: Protecting Tefillin During Travel or Risk of Loss Permits Continued Wearing Even After Nightfall
Halacha Without Broadcasting: Rav Ashi's Example of Acting on Lenient Halakhic Ground While Not Publicizing It to Prevent Laxity and Sleep-Related Transgressions
The Constant Mashmesh Requirement: Comparing Tefillin to the Tzitz and the Imperative of Continuous Mental Awareness of God's Presence

macro_segments.title: On Which Hand: The Debate Over Whether Tefillin Goes on the Left Hand (Yad) or the Weak Hand (Yad Keha)

micro_segments.title: The Etymology Dispute: Does Yad Mean Left Hand Objectively, or Does Yad Keha (Weak Hand) Allow for Variation Based on Handedness
Rabbi Natan's Approach: Deriving Placement from the Parallel Obligation to Bind and Write, Establishing That Binding Mimics Writing, Which Is Done with the Right Hand
Rabbi Akiva's Derivation: The Spelling Variant Yad with Hey Rather Than Chaf, Indicating Weak Hand and Creating a Semantic Basis for Understanding Yad as Specifically the Weak Arm
The Lefty Question: Abaye's Resolution That Ambidextrous or Ambivalent Cases Default to the Conventional Left Hand, Not the Subjectively Weak Hand
Tosafot's Nuance: Balancing Keshartem (Binding with Right Hand) Against Yad Keha (Weak Hand); How Different Dominances Affect Ruling
The Conceptual Shift: Whether Tefillin Observance Emphasizes the Action of Binding (Keshartem) or the State of Wearing, and How This Reframes Which Hand Matters

macro_segments.title: Missing or Disabled Limbs: The Halakhic Status of One Who Lacks a Proper Arm or Hand for Tefillin

micro_segments.title: The Gidem Question: One Who Lacks a Hand But Retains the Upper Arm; Tannaitic Debate on Whether This Person Is Exempt or Still Obligated
Rema's Ruling: Placing Tefillin Without Bracha on the Remaining Limb; The Mishnah Berurah's Clarification on What Constitutes a Viable Placement Location
Complete Loss of the Weak Arm: The Pivot to Shel Rosh; How One Can Still Fulfill the Mitzva Fully Using Head Tefillin with Both Brachot
Rav Moshe's Clarification: Distinguishing Between Permanent Limb Loss, Paralysis, and Atrophy; The Role of Blood Flow in Determining If a Limb Counts as Present
The Touching Testimony: Rav Moshe's Father Wearing Tefillin on His Paralyzed Left Arm During His Final Illness; The Halakhic Principle That Presence with Loss of Function Still Obligates

macro_segments.title: The Placement on the Body: How Scriptural Comparisons Establish the Precise Location for Both Arm and Head Tefillin

micro_segments.title: On the Hand: Yad Refers to the Upper Arm (Kiboret), Not the Palm, Derived by Comparison to How the Head Placement Is Located at Height
Two Heads Thought Experiment: The Gemara's Humorous Exchange with Plimo and Later with a Real Case of Bifida; Why the Answer Remains Unresolved
On the Head: The Location at Height (Govah) Where Hair Grows, Not Literally Between the Eyes; Comparison to the Tufa (Bald Spot) Prohibition

macro_segments.title: Visibility and Privacy: Whether Tefillin Must Be Covered and the Principle of Lecha L'Ot (A Sign for You, Not for Others)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Visibility Argues Against Low Placement; The Principle That Signs Are for Personal Reminder, Not Public Display
The Practical Result: Sleeve-Pulling and Plastic Boxes; Minhag to Cover Tefillin Based on Lecha L'Ot, Though Not Strictly Mandated
Rav Ashi's Wound Story: Even When Tefillin Become Visible Due to Injury, Amemar Clarifies That Coverage Is a Location Principle, Not a Strict Obligation

macro_segments.title: The Placement on the Body: How Scriptural Comparisons Establish the Precise Location for Both Arm and Head Tefillin (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehuda's Alternative: Using Negaim Terminology to Define Placement as Areas Where Single-Category Skin or Hair Lesions Occur, Excluding Eyebrow Region

macro_segments.title: Transition and Conclusion: Completing the Study of Tefillin and Previewing the Next Topic of Tzitzit

micro_segments.title: Summary of Tefillin Discussion: Key Themes About Time, Placement, Awareness, and the Distinction Between Mitzvot of Action and Mitzvot of State
Preview of Perek Daled: The Opening Mishnah on Tzitzit, Introducing the Framework That Certain Components Are Not Me'akev (Interdependent) in the Fulfillment of This Mitzva

topical_tags.term: Layla Lav Zman Tefillin (Night is not a time for tefillin)
Halacha v'Ein Morin Kein (this is the halacha but we don't teach it)
Lecha L'Ot (a sign for you, not for others)
